question.text: A put option in an AIF investment agreement allows the AIF to:

answer.text: Purchase additional shares of the investee company
Force the promoter or company to buy back the AIF's investment at a predetermined price after a specified period
Sell shares on the stock exchange immediately
Terminate the fund before its tenure ends

explanation.text: **The Logic:** A put option gives the AIF the right (but not obligation) to sell its shares back to the promoter or the company at a predetermined price after a specified period. This provides an exit mechanism when other exit routes (IPO, strategic sale) are not available. **The Trap:** A put option is a right, not an obligation. The AIF may choose not to exercise it if better exit options become available. The enforceability of put options has been a legal gray area in India. **Cross-Exam Context:** Exit mechanisms including put options are tested in XIX-A and XIX-C. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Put options provide downside protection but their enforceability can be challenged. Evaluate whether the put option has adequate legal backing and whether the counterparty (promoter/company) has the financial capacity to honor it.
